title: The Slow Road Visualisation
subtitle: A five-minute visualisation for when steady forward movement needs a picture to hold
excerpt: A five-minute visualisation for when the slow pace of body work needs a concrete image to rest in. The picture is a road taken calmly, not rushed. Used when the urge to overhaul is loud and a quieter frame is wanted.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Slow-road imagery is a staple of somatic self-regulation practice.

practice.whenToUse: Use when the fix-it-fast urge is loud and a slower frame is needed to start the day without the overhaul pressure.

content.text: A five-minute visualisation for when the body work needs a different picture. The sprint picture keeps showing up. This visualisation replaces it with a road walked at a sensible pace.
New to visualisation as a practice? Read
Sit or lie down. Close your eyes. Take three slow breaths, breathing out longer than you breathe in.

Picture a road. Not a motorway. A country road, wide enough for two people walking side by side. The surface is firm. The light is the kind that does not change much. There is no arrival point visible from where you are standing.
Picture yourself on that road. You are walking at a pace that does not cost you anything. You are not late. You are not early. The road is yours. The pace is yours.

Notice that you are moving. The road behind you is different from the road ahead, and both are the same road. Each step adds to the distance covered. No single step is the one that changes everything.
Stay with the road for three to five minutes. When you open your eyes, the plan for the day stays at this pace.
Original to homemade.education. Slow-road imagery comes from somatic practice.
